data.title: The Money Supply Flatlines As Employment Cools And Delinquencies Rise
data.description:
By Ryan McMaken

The money supply has nearly flatlined in 2025, with July’s total money supply increasing by only $39 billion over the past seven months. The total size of the money supply still remains more than $5 trillion above its pre-covid total - an increase of 35 percent - but trends in delinquencies, employment, and home sales have put downward pressure on the money supply throughout much of 2025.

As of July, year-over-year growth in the money supply was at 2.45 percent. That’s down slightly from June’s year-over-year increase of 2.49 percent. Money supply growth is also up compared to July of last year, when year-over-year growth was -0.43 percent.

The money supply has now increased, year over year, for twelve months in a row, following a very volatile period of immense growth in the money supply - i.e., during 2020 and 2021 - followed by eighteen months of sizable declines in the money supply during 2023 and 2024. Since then, however, money supply trends have largely flattened.

This trend has been especially true in the first half of 2025. Month over month, the money supply in July was up by 0.13 percent, rising only slightly from June’s month-to-month growth rate of -0.17 percent. During July of last year, the month-to-month growth rate was 0.17 percent.

The total money supply has hovered around $19.3 trillion for the past three months, and is little changed from January of this year, rising 0.2 percent, or $39 billion since then.

This flattening of monetary growth likely reflects several trends we now see in the economy. For example, recent employment reports have shown that job growth has largely stalled, and July’s revisions to employment totals suggest that employment trends are worse than was previously thought.

Moreover, recent reports from the Federal Reserve Bank of New York show that delinquencies on student loans, auto loans, and credit cards are all at or above the levels they were during the Great Recession. Meanwhile, bankruptcies are up by more than 11 percent in 2025 compared to last year, and pending home sales continue to fall.

All of these trends are likely to have an effect on money supply growth. As Frank Shostak has explained, the money supply will often grow along with loan activity at commercial banks, and increases in loan activity requires an abundance of credit-worthy borrowers.

As delinquencies and bankruptcies rise - a trend made worse by stagnating employment - the number of available borrowers falls. Fewer loans will then be made, and the money supply will grow less swiftly.

On the other end, a growing number of defaulting borrowers means previously lent dollars will “disappear” as these loans are never paid back, putting downward pressure on the overall money supply. As home sale totals fall, this also tends to mean fewer newly lent dollars entering the economy.

All of this together is a recipe for diminishing growth in the money supply. When combined with the Fed’s recent reluctance to lower the target policy interest rate - which has meant fewer monetary injections from the Fed’s open market operations - we’re now seeing a flattening in the overall money supply.

The money supply metric used here - the “true,” or Rothbard-Salerno, money supply measure (TMS) - is the metric developed by Murray Rothbard and Joseph Salerno, and is designed to provide a better measure of money supply fluctuations than M2.

Historically, M2 growth rates have often followed a similar course to TMS growth rates, but M2 is now growing considerably faster than TMS, and a gap between the two measures has gradually widened.

In July, the M2 growth rate, year over year, was 4.79 percent. That’s up from June’s growth rate of 4.51 percent. July’s growth rate was also up from July 2024’s rate of 1.54 percent. Month over month, M2 has also grown faster than TMS, rising by 0.33 percent from June to July.

Although year-over-year and month-to-month growth rates may be moderating, money supply totals remain far above what they were before 2020 and the covid panic. From 2020 to 2022, the Federal Reserve’s easy-money policies resulted in approximately 6.4 trillion dollars being added to the economy.

This was done to help finance the federal government’s enormous deficits driven by runaway covid stimulus programs. Although the total size of the money supply has fallen since mid-2022, it has certainly not fallen enough to return money supply growth to the pre-covid trend, and the total money supply as of July remains five trillion above where it was at the beginning of 2020.

Since 2009, the TMS money supply is now up by nearly 194 percent. (M2 has grown by nearly 156 percent in that period.) Out of the current money supply of $19.3 trillion, nearly 26 percent of that has been created since January 2020.

Since 2009, in the wake of the global financial crisis, more than $12 trillion of the current money supply has been created. In other words, nearly two-thirds of the total existing money supply have been created just in the past thirteen years.

As a result, money supply is now well above the former trend that was in place before 2020. For example, just to get back to the money-creation trend that existed in 2019 before the “great covid inflation,” total money supply would need to fall by at least three trillion dollars.

Why Is There a Growing Gap Between M2 and TMS?

But why is there a growing gap between M2 and TMS, and does it mean anything? To answer the first part of this question, we need to consider how M2 is calculated differently from TMS. First, TMS includes Treasury deposits at Federal Reserve banks, while M2 does not.

These deposits have fallen by nearly $200 billion since January. This has brought down TMS totals, but has not directly reduced M2. Moreover, M2 does include retail money funds, while TMS does not include these funds as money.

So, with retail money funds increasing by more than $100 billion since January, we’re seeing more upward pressure on M2 than on TMS. These are just two factors that are helping to form an ongoing gap between M2 and TMS.

Does this gap matter? Historically, a large and growing gap between M2 and TMS has suggested in several cases that the US economy has already entered a recession. Looking back to the months before the onset of recession in 1990, 2001, and 2007, we find that the gap between TMS and M2 was near a new peak in each case.

A similar phenomenon happened in late 2019 along with the repo crisis of that year. This was likely a prelude to a 2020 recession that would have happened had the central bank not intervened to push extreme levels of new liquidity during the covid panic. Now, in mid-2025, this gap is now the largest it has been in decades - and possibly ever.

This doesn’t prove that the US is in the midst of a recession at the moment, but the growing gap between M2 and TMS suggests that the US is, at least, entering a period of serious economic weakness.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Build-A-Bear Q2: Expanding Earnings Internationally As Brand Visibility Grows
data.description:
Build-A-Bear Workshop, Inc. (NYSE:BBW) reported the company’s fiscal Q2 results on the 28th of August. The plush animal retailer showed impressive momentum, sending the stock up 14% for the day. Build-A-Bear showed solid financials all around with brick-and-mortar and e-commerce momentum and continued franchise expansion. Increased brand visibility has allowed Build-A-Bear to continue expanding the franchise on a global scale.

I downgraded my rating on the stock to Hold in my previous December 2024 article, titled “Build-A-Bear: Strong Expansion Prospects, But A Disappointing Q4 Outlook”. The downgrade seems to have been too early – Build-A-Bear has since returned 45%, well above the S&P 500’s 7% return.

Build-A-Bear Reported Great Q2 Financials as the Brand Gains Ground

The fiscal Q2 report was significantly good. Revenues reached $124.2 million with 11.1% growth, reflecting the franchise’s continued expansion - year-on-year, corporately-managed stores grew by 7 to 368 locations, partner-operated stores by 50 to 157 locations, and franchise locations by 22 to a total of 102 stores from Q2’FY24. Retail sales grew by 10.8% to $114.6 million with strong direct-to-consumer momentum in especially ecommerce, and commercial and international franchise revenues grew by 15.2% to $9.6 million amid continued global expansion. Build-A-Bear’s brick-and-mortar traffic was also strong with a 3% increase against an uncertain macroeconomic backdrop.

The topline result beat Wall Street’s consensus by $8.1 million.

Alongside strong sales momentum, Build-A-Bear showed strong pricing power. The gross margin improved by 340 basis points, reflecting lower promotional activity, price increases, and sales leverage – Build-A-Bear’s brand is performing very strongly, enabling an improved average selling price and operating leverage. Reflectingly, pre-tax income came in at $15.3 million, up by 32.7% year-on-year at a 12.3% margin. Higher store-level compensation, corporate costs, and inflationary pressure caused a 140 basis point margin headwind from SG&A, offsetting a part of gross margin gains. Regardless, total profitability was incredibly good, and the quarter’s $0.94 EPS beat Wall Street’s consensus by $0.27. The Q2 double beat underlines a solid financial performance, ultimately stemming from Build-A-Bear’s strengthening brand, digital transformation, and global franchise expansion.

Build-A-Bear slightly raised the FY2025 pre-tax income guidance to a $62-70 million range. The guidance suggests stagnant earnings from $67.1 million in FY2024 due to a mostly transitory $11 million tariff headwind – pricing increases and other mitigation measures should offset most tariff pressure in the future. The longer-term earnings growth outlook remains great through Build-A-Bear’s well-performing and expanding brand.

Build-A-Bear’s Fast Global Expansion Continues with a Guidance Raise

Notably, Build-A-Bear raised the FY2025 store expansion outlook. The company now assumes at least 50-60 openings through the year, an improvement from at least 50 openings expected previously and suggesting a good continuation from 64 openings in FY2024. Domestic partners have continued to open stores within the U.S. as discussed in the Q2 earnings call, while international growth has been even stronger. Global partners and franchisees recently entered Georgia and Uzbekistan as new markets as Build-A-Bear is now eyeing a re-entry to Germany in Q3 alongside openings in existing international markets.

The higher expansion outlook is great – the partner and franchisee -based growth model is very capital light, highlighted by Build-A-Bear’s low capital expenditures. The business model brings Build-A-Bear quite predictable earnings growth with low investments.

Expansion seems to have gained ground through Build-A-Bear’s increased social media marketing – campaigns across social media platforms have elevated Build-A-Bear’s brand visibility, bolstering e-commerce growth and trickling into brick-and-mortar traffic. Further, social media campaigns have very wide global reach, and Build-A-Bear’s brand equity has clearly reached a global basis through social media as the franchise is expanding especially fast in international markets.

BBW Stock's Valuation Reflects a Good Amount of Growth Already

I updated my discounted cash flow (DCF) model for Build-A-Bear. I now assume growth to continue across the business, resulting in a FY2024-FY2034 revenue CAGR of 4.1% with elevated growth in the next few years.

While tariffs add significant short-term margin pressure, I assume tariff mitigation and growth to rebound margins to a new high. I now assume the EBIT margin to stabilize at 14%, and that Build-A-Bear will continue to convert after-tax earnings well into free cash flow through the capital light growth strategy.

The estimates put Build-A-Bear’s fair value at $67.3, near the stock price at the time of writing. The stock has continued to rise in price, already reflecting improved base scenario growth expectations. Build-A-Bear would have to drive significant long-term franchise expansion for the stock to still generate outsized returns.

The fair value estimate is up from $52.5 previously.

CAPM

The DCF model uses a cost of capital [WACC] of 9.29%, derived from a capital asset pricing model:

I don’t assume Build-A-Bear to draw debt. For cost of equity, I use the 10-year bond yield of 4.22% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 4.62% is Professor Aswath Damodaran’s most recent July estimate for the US. I now use Aswath Damodaran’s 0.99 unlevered beta estimate for the general retail. With a liquidity premium of 0.5%, the cost of equity and WACC both stand at 9.29%.

Takeaway

Build-A-Bear’s Q2 report was great as the brand continues to gain ground through global franchise expansion. Past transitory tariff pressure, the earnings outlook stands good as social media visibility has created a platform for international growth. Ultimately, the stock’s valuation already reflects a good amount of future growth, making BBW stock’s risk-to-reward balanced. I maintain a Hold rating for now.

data.title: A2Z Cust2Mate Solutions Smart Carts Could Redefine Retail, But At A Price
data.description:
The shares of A2Z Cust2Mate (NASDAQ:AZ) have been moving along with the completion of a series of execution milestones and capital raises. The stock has soared more than 470% over the past year and nearly 45% in 2025. This bull run comes on the heels of some new major development, which drove the stock higher.

Recent developments include an oversized June 2025 order ($25 million to 3,000 Cust2Mate 3.0 carts), strategic payments partnership with Nayax, and a January 2025 follow-on, which injected about $30 million of fresh capital into the firm- all of which will trigger recurring, multi-year software revenues tied to 3-year contracts per cart deployed.

The combination of those transactions, a clean balance sheet (it would have had about $36 million cash on hand by mid-2025), little to no long-term debt, and at least one analyst with a $20 12-month target helps to explain why the stock is trading on premium multiples (EV/Sales 39.7x) today. The market certainly has high hopes about top-line growth.

The financial model has also shown that the speed of deployment + recurring SaaS revenue per cart, a large amount of revenue visibility over the next several years, even in case profitability does not reach until then.

However, the company valuation is extremely rich, and the company is still burning cash, which is why I am assigning a "Hold" rating on the stock despite some positive developments.

Company Introduction

A2Z Cust2Mate Solutions Corp is a technology company that sells “smart” self-checkout shopping carts to retailers. Its flagship Cust2Mate cart adds a touchscreen, computer vision, and an integrated scanner/payment system so customers can simply “pick & go” without cashier lines.

The smart cart streams data to store servers and collects a per-unit subscription fee, blending upfront hardware sales with recurring software revenue. Based in Israel/Canada (formerly A2Z Smart Tech), A2Z partners with grocers and payment platforms (e.g., a recent tie-up with Nayax) to deploy its Cust2Mate 3.0 carts. Its goal is to improve retail efficiency and shopper experience by digitizing the checkout process.

Financial Overview

In the financial history of AZ, one is really able to witness a company that is in ramp-up mode. Revenue was a few million in 2020 and increased in 2021 to around $3 million, and in 2023, it increased to $11 million. This was not a steady growth, though.

However, that same year, the sales fell once more to the level of around $7.2 million, or by 37%, in comparison with the previous year. The year-to-year changes are somewhat steep: in 2022, compared to 2021, the revenue increased considerably (by nearly threefold), and in 2023, the firm saw a less significant increase (by almost 22%) as it continued its expansion.

However, that growth slowed much later; in 2024, the change in revenue was achieved at a rate of 36% reduction. Such a bumpy trend is explained by the presence of project timing and the variations in international rollout. Margin has been creeping up gradually, but the margin is narrow. In Q4 2024, the gross margin is 30% versus 18% in 2020, and profitability remains at a long distance.

The net income has been negative on a yearly basis. In 2021, it made a loss of $39 million, $16 million in 2022 and 2023, and $17 million in 2024.

On the balance sheet, it looks like things are getting better. At the end of 2023, the cash on hand fell to only $2 million, but rose to $14 million at the end of 2024, then rose again to about $36 million by the middle of 2025 after new funding. Debt is not much of a concern, as the long-term debt-to-asset ratio has more or less been zero since 2021. The liabilities are approximately $12 million, but pro-forma equity is currently above zero at $17.9 million in the late 2024 period. Although the company continues to run at a net loss, it is now much better cushioned after the third-line financing.

Fast forward, in Q1 2025, AZ earned revenue of $1.97 million, a 16% growth YoY, but it had an operating loss of $7.53 million as it incurred build-out expenses.

By mid-2025, A2Z had approximately $36.0 million in cash. The monthly cash burn based on the H1 2025 operating loss of $13.37 million is approximately $2.23 million, which means that the runway will be approximately 16 months at the present spending. When we instead take the 2024 full-year net loss $18.5 million, the implied runway is approximately 25 months. In an aggressive case, there is a 50% increased burn due to deployment and build-out, and the runway drops to approximately 11 months.

These estimates will be based on no new financings, material working-capital changes, or immediate large contract receipts; early SaaS revenue would significantly lengthen the runway, and timing of payments would also be a factor.

New investments in its FastView digital advertising platform are the current priority, and variants of continuing delivery to be married to recurring charges rather than product sales are in process.

In summary, the revenues have been erratic (early rapid growth, recent decline), losses are large, but cash is significant, and debt is insignificant. They must ask: can new orders and foreign rollout reverse top-line contraction (shouldering significant research and development fatigue may begin to bear fruit)?

Some Major Developments

Further parameters that have contributed the most to the ever-industrial momentum in the stock of AZ in 2024-2025 would also entail a huge foreign contract, Trixo acquiring a mega tissue order in Latin America/Mexico.

In June 2025, Trixo signed a 25-million-cart (3,000 Cust2Mate carts) order with A2Z. These carts will be operated based on a recurring 36-month structure of a recurrent fee, which means predictable incomes as soon as 2026. This huge order is only the preliminary phase of a transaction with Trixo, and the management feels that the same category of orders will continue to be witnessed in the future.

In Sept 2024, A2Z partnered with Nayax, integrating Nayax on-cart payment devices into Cust2Mate carts. The first application is underway in France and a goal of tens of thousands of carts worldwide is aimed. CEO Gadi Graus said that this is going to expand our sales territory and position retail sales. In Aug 2024, a pilot launch at the Franprix chain (600+ stores) proved to have real-world traction.

A2Z made a few capital rounds in late 2024 and early 2025 ($12.5 million in Dec 2024 and a $30 million follow-on in Jan 2025. This cash added to its treasury increased its war chest of funds. Following such financings, post-offering cash is 34-fold the amount prior to the offering. This implies that the business firm has liquidity (and even an asset-based credit facility). cust2mate.com This gives the company more funding capability to expand and meet its backlog without any dilution in the near future. The share price responded well to all capital raises and orders: it rose on each occasion.

All but one of the Wall Street analysts who cover AZ have assigned a "Strong Buy" rating to the stock with a $20 target price (116% upside). Such an ambitious goal demonstrates faith in the recurring-revenue model and international growth. And in brief, AZ has the makings of a fully loaded pipeline: new customer logos and order backlog (Catalyst), adequate cash reserves and debt capacity (finance); and zeal (Valuation). In case of deployments, every successful deployment should send the stock up.

Valuation and Risks

AZ has a bullish forecast, but it is associated with high risk. The company is not profitable and cash ingesting: it experiences persistent losses and has negative cash flows, and it relies on external finances to continue. Execution risk remains critical here unless smart carts are able to multiply quickly across stores. High variable costs will keep producing losses. Such a slowdown of adoption might be easier to achieve when grocers begin to reduce technology investments or when they experience delays in supply chains. The forward P/S is highly valued.

Trailing P/S ratio is extremely high, about 36x (market cap $320-330 million against TTM revenue $7.5 million), the forward P/S is 19.5x, which means that the market is priced on significant growth in revenues. A forward P/S around 19x (and a sky-high PEG ratio) reflects a very bullish assessment of accelerated scale and margin growth; unless top line growth continues to accelerate beyond current exuberant expectations, the current valuation seems excessive relative to peers, unless scale and margin expansion rates are higher than market consensus would imagine, and therefore any signs of slowdown could lead to a steep decline in the share price. There is also competitive risk (other self-checkout technology, Amazon (AMZN), NCR, and so on).

Put simply, A2Z is a reconnaissance sort of project, and in that it could succeed well, and fail quite badly, particularly in the case of funding, regulatory roadbumps, integration, or security.

Conclusion

A2Z Cust2Mate offers an innovative retail platform: a proven smart-cart solution with recurring-fee revenue potential. Its stock trades on big forward assumptions (analysts peg a $20 target), and 2025’s stock gains reflect tangible progress on those assumptions.

The recent order wins, partnerships, and strong cash position support the bull case. However, profitability is far off; the company must keep raising funds or vastly boost sales. As of mid-2025, I maintain my "Hold" stance on the stock of AZ, which could reward investors if its rollouts hit forecasts, but the growth story is priced in. The forward P/S and PEG show the market is banking on exponential growth.

In sum, AZ is a high-risk/high-reward idea: exciting if execution succeeds, very risky if it doesn’t. So it's a "Hold" for me now.

data.title: Panasonic's New Bets Look Likely To Pay Off
data.description:
Panasonic Holdings Corporation (OTCPK:PCRFF) (OTCPK:PCRHY) is at the crossroads. Its transformation efforts over the past decade have culminated in current reorganization that aims for a leaner, more profitable, and more valuable Panasonic. These changes could not come soon enough for the long-suffering shareholders.

Latest financials

Financial results for Q1 of fiscal 2026 (from April to June 2025) reveal a picture reflective of the transition that the conglomerate is undergoing.

Excluding Automotive Systems (in which Panasonic retains a minority stake of 20%), overall sales increased by 2%, with all segments ending in positive territory. Operating profit increased by 3% even including the deconsolidation impact; net profit added 1.8%. However, cash flows, down 21% year-on-year, did not escape unscathed.

As anticipated, notable results came from AI-related businesses in Industry and Energy as well as parts of Connect like Process Automation and Mobile Solutions. These happen to be the same segments that are in focus moving forward — those offering B2B solutions in growth areas such as automotive batteries and supply chain management software.

Business reforms

The Group Management Reform, announced in February this year, is arguably the most significant restructuring exercise since Panasonic became a holding company in 2022. The immediate goal is cost cutting; that will include eliminating 10,000 jobs in Japan and abroad within a year’s time.

Efficiency is being sought in other ways too. Consumer electronics subsidiaries of Lifestyle, the largest segment (with 44% of total revenue), will turn into separate operating companies starting next fiscal year. Cost measures such as these are expected to contribute heavily toward the initial target of adding ¥150 billion ($1 billion) to profit by FY2027.

Returns from expansion and investment in priority businesses — such as energy solutions — are to be realised more gradually. Fiscal 2026, in fact, may see a ‘temporary’ decline in ROE, from last recorded 7.9% to about 6.5%, given major restructuring expenses (¥130 billion, or $900 million). The eventual target for ROE is 10% in three years' time.

Focus areas

Panasonic’s long-termism is evident in the fact that it has chosen to zero in for growth on Solutions represented by Connect, a segment which at present makes up only 16% of total revenue. Other segments — including Lifestyle that is undergoing major product changes — are supposed to play the supportive role of bolstering the profit base.

While Lifestyle is objectively struggling amid intense competition, there is greater hope for Industry and Energy. Energy, in particular, has the highest profitability, with a 10% operating margin. This is reason enough for Panasonic to keep pouring resources into new technology and production capacity for automotive batteries and energy solutions.

There are few concerns stymieing the conglomerate’s enthusiasm. An imminent slowdown in the electric vehicle market, primarily in North America, Panasonic’s established base, has led to softer growth rate forecasts for Energy. Any shortfall though is expected to be made up by robust demand for AI-enabled data center energy storage systems.

Risks

The forecast for full FY2026 is all negative percentage change wise. But the reasons have to do more with business reorganization and less with actual performance. Sales should actually increase if deconsolidated Automotive is excluded from comparison. Profit figures will be affected by restructuring costs.

Although the one risk with the biggest element of the unknown has not been factored in. Panasonic seems comfortable discounting the impact of US tariffs — and on solid ground. The supply chain is diversified to begin with, but it also stands to benefit from additional business and tax credit as a US-based producer of Energy devices.

Valuation

At a P/E of 9.8x and EV/EBITDA of 5.4x, principal Tokyo-listed Panasonic (TSE:6752) looks as affordable as ever. To be sure, over the past year the stock returned way more than the Japanese market, 33% versus 17%. But longer-term returns are exactly reversed, hence the pent-up frustration among shareholders.

Still, better late than never. The market is viewing this latest iteration of Panasonic rather positively. Even with a lower earnings forecast, analyst price targets have been holding relatively steady. The average 12-month target is more than 30% above the current share price. Dividend yield is growing and is a well-covered 3.7% right now.

Conclusion

Panasonic may be finally coming out of its shell. The management has been pretty outspoken recently, to reassure investors that raising profitability is priority number one. Focus on Connect for future growth and core specialized capabilities in Energy and Industry for profit (more details on this during Investor Day in December) makes for quite a compelling case. It helps that Panasonic has been explicit about how long it would take to see the results, for they will not be immediately apparent; the end goal is to add ¥300 billion to profit by FY2029. Shareholders, prepare to wait some more.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: 2025 Fall Investment Directions: Rethinking Diversification
data.description:
By Gargi Pal Chaudhuri, Kristy Akullian, CFA

Transcript

Kristy Akullian - The foundational relationships that once anchored traditional portfolio construction are shifting — possibly making many traditional portfolios riskier overall. We believe this market regime may persist, with potentially profound implications for investors.

Hi, I’m Kristy Akullian, Head of iShares Investment Strategy, here with our top investment considerations for fall.

  1. Investor sentiment has remained strong despite macro and geopolitical uncertainty - although we are seeing meaningful shifts in how investors have been building portfolios. We believe investors should consider being more deliberate in their search for diversification, perhaps considering alternative asset classes and strategies alongside traditional holdings like stocks and bonds.
  2. We keep our preference for the 3-7 year belly of the curve in fixed income as we anticipate the Fed will begin cutting rates again in September but ultimately maintain moderately restrictive policy.
  3. We remain broadly constructive on U.S. growth equities, but favor a selective and nimble approach as macro data potentially softens.
  4. A declining U.S. dollar has helped boost international returns, potentially indicating a structural relationship change that may require investors to reconsider the role of international equities in a portfolio.

Our full outlook lays out the macro backdrop and explores the potential opportunities across U.S. and international equities, fixed income, and ways to think about portfolio construction.

Thanks for watching. Check out the full report for specific product ideas designed to help you navigate the current regime.

A Big Shift In Portfolio Construction?

Modern portfolios are changing

Markets surged following publication of our Spring Investment Directions, and investor sentiment has remained robust despite ongoing macroeconomic and geopolitical uncertainty.1 While we remain broadly constructive on U.S. equities, we believe that traditional portfolios may be riskier overall because of significant changes to foundational relationships2, including stock-bond correlations and the “safe haven” status of the U.S. dollar.

At the heart of asset allocation decisions lies the textbook relationship that stocks and bonds have had a negative correlation, meaning when stocks go down as company prospects deteriorate, investors often turn to bonds in search of safer assets. This has remained the core underpinning of a traditional portfolio’s most basic asset allocation breakdown.

But we believe this relationship has fundamentally shifted; less reliable correlations undermine the diversification benefits the two core asset classes provided each other.

Unlike previous episodes of temporary correlation spikes, we believe today’s alignment between stocks and bonds, as shown in Figure 1, reflects deeper structural forces: persistent inflation dynamics, policy action and fiscal imbalances. This all suggests this regime may endure and fundamentally alter portfolio risk profiles.

Figure 1: Positive stock/bond correlation has been persistent

Other, subtler, relationships appear to be shifting as well, further heightening the potential for risk. U.S. investors have long benefited from an overweight to domestic equities, but with an increased risk premium on the U.S. dollar, portfolios may benefit from exposure to international equities and digital assets. Within equity sleeves, the rise of AI has also meant a rise in U.S. index concentration, creating a need to source diversification elsewhere.3 Falling rates and sticky inflation create a challenge for investors seeking income, prompting us to consider short-dated TIPS and equity income as inflation conscious sources of cash flows. And within fixed income allocations, we feel how a portfolio sources duration is as important as how much there is overall, arguing for more active yield curve management than traditional benchmarks can offer.

As investors rethink their portfolio allocations, we believe there is room to carve out space for non-traditional approaches and exposures. We feel a mix of digital assets, income strategies and international equities can help improve portfolio diversification. (Learn more about the potential diversification benefits of bitcoin and gold.)

What’s The Outlook For U.S. Equities?

‎Our macro outlook expects U.S. economic momentum to slow and inflation to be sticky, but we remain broadly constructive on U.S. equities and favor a selective and nimble approach as macro data potentially softens.

We anticipate the Fed to resume their easing cycle and gradually reduce overnight interest rates by 25 basis points starting in September.4 As front end rates move lower, we believe investors should seek income elsewhere. U.S. households held nearly $20 trillion in cash and other liquid assets in the first half of the year, and they’ve allocated more to cash than to stocks or bonds over the last three years.5

With some of that cash potentially moving into stocks, our view supports U.S. growth equities over value, based on an exceptionally strong fundamental outlook for U.S. tech companies benefitting from the ongoing AI capex wave.6

For the first time since the “Magnificent 7” term took hold, the cohort of mega-cap tech stocks underperformed broad U.S. equities in H1. But the Mag 7's 11% year-to-date return masks a 50% spread between top and bottom performers, potentially pointing to a new phase in the AI trade: market leadership is splintering, even at the top, as investors question legacy business models.7

We believe this fractured leadership justifies an active approach to AI. The iShares A.I. Innovation and Tech Active ETF (BAI) is an actively managed strategy that aims to maximize total return by investing in high-conviction opportunities across the AI tech stack. Leveraging active insights, BAI dynamically navigates the evolving AI landscape seeking emerging growth potential.

Calls for an end or plateau to AI capex growth have been persistent, but we believe there is still plenty of room for spending.8 McKinsey estimates that by 2030, $5.2T will be invested in AI datacenters across chips/hardware, power and land.9 Estimates indicate roughly $320B was spent on AI datacenters in 2023 and 2024 combined.10

Tech and communication services have continued to lead in earnings beats and forward guidance, and we believe AI will continue to be the growth engine of U.S. equity markets. We also like tactically rotating to select sectors where fundamentals have remained robust. Financials remain our highest-conviction example where broadening out has taken shape. U.S. financials, particularly banks, have benefitted from regulatory tailwinds, sticky inflation and a backlog of investment banking activity: all themes reinforced by Q2 earnings commentary.

Equity market leadership necessitates a more granular and tactical approach to factor investing. For factor investing strategies, we still like quality at this stage in the economic cycle, but this year’s rapidly shifting sentiment and positioning indicators leave us with a preference for active rotation solutions. The iShares U.S. Equity Factor Rotation Active ETF (DYNF) employs such a strategy and aims to outperform a market cap-weighted benchmark across a variety of market environments.

Are expensive valuations justified?

Amid our preference for U.S. stocks, questions have consistently emerged about expensive valuations.

History shows that valuations have not been strong predictors of near-term performance. The market continues to clear all-time highs and currently trades at a 12% premium to its 5-year average forward price-to-earnings ratio.11 Since 2000, the correlation between the S&P 500’s forward P/E and its subsequent 6-month return has been negative 0.21, suggesting the relationship is weak.12

Further, while valuations may be high relative to historical averages, year-to-date returns for tech and growth companies appear to come from earnings growth — not multiple expansion. Valuations have been responsible for less than 4% of returns for these cohorts, as shown in Figure 2.

Figure 2: U.S. tech returns have been driven primarily by earnings

Sources of return YTD

Importantly, we believe that as long as valuations remain supported by superior earnings growth, the market can sustain these levels. Our takeaways from second-quarter earnings reinforce that strength: it was one of the strongest seasons for earnings beats on record. Equally encouraging, 58% of companies raised full-year EPS guidance, double the pace seen in the first quarter.13

While we don’t prefer using rich valuations as a sell signal, we also caution against using cheap ones as an indicator to buy. U.S. small caps currently trade at a discount versus historical averages.14 But even as the Fed is expected to begin easing, we maintain our caution to small caps, which face twin headwinds:

  • Historically greater sensitivity to slowing economic growth, and;
  • Fundamentals that remain soft, with net margins not expected to return to pre-pandemic highs until 2026.15

What’s Driving International Stocks?

‎International equities have led the way in gains this year, although many U.S. investors may have missed these higher returns. Home country bias may be costing investors: The average advisor allocates 77.5% of their equity portfolio to the U.S., up from 70% in 2018.16 Heightened international returns follow a supportive mix of fiscal and monetary spending abroad, a weaker dollar, and the possibility of interest rate reductions in the U.S. International equities can help build a well-diversified portfolio, particularly as they show potential for continued outperformance from dollar weakness. (Learn more about the outperformance of international stocks.)

Investors have started to correct for this underweight and are turning to international equities. Non-U.S. equity exposures now comprise 29% of total equity ETF flow YTD vs. 12% last year.17 We believe there are key reasons why there’s a strong appetite for non-U.S. exposure.

A declining dollar has historically lifted returns. We believe we are toward the beginning of a weaker dollar cycle, which has tended to boost international returns — potentially indicating a structural relationship change that requires investors to consider evolving portfolio construction. The dollar has long benefited from a cycle of robust U.S. growth and investment. But we think the structural bull market may be challenged amid current trade policy and demand for alternative reserve currencies.

Historical evidence underpins our view that foreign exchange moves in sustained, longer-term cycles: Since 1971, we’ve observed six completed dollar cycles, with an average duration of about eight years (Figure 3). If we are at the beginning of a longer-term cycle of dollar weakness, we see reason to reconfigure portfolios. Unhedged international equities may stand to benefit the most from ongoing currency weakness.

Figure 3: We’re still early in the FX cycle

Length of U.S. dollar cycles since 1971

International equities have proven to be better diversifiers to U.S. large caps than the small-cap overweight most financial advisors hold.18

That relationship has been emphasized in drawdown periods: since 2010, when the S&P 500 posted negative quarterly returns, small caps underperformed significantly — posting nearly double the losses of developed markets.19

Fixed Income: Where Are The Opportunities?

‎We anticipate the Fed to resume their easing cycle and gradually reduce overnight interest rates by 25 basis points starting in September.20 Until there is more certainty on the passthrough of tariffs to consumer prices and the composition of the FOMC, [among other issues,] we believe that the market may test the Fed’s resolve to ease by pricing firmer rates on the long end of the yield curve and wider inflation expectations.

As stock-bond correlations diverge rapidly, investors have changed where they source duration and diversification. Given the current correlation backdrop, we maintain our preference for the 3- to 7-year 'belly' of the yield curve. In our opinion, this rapid divergence also necessitates a more active oversight, even of core index holdings. ETF flows suggest that investors are abandoning the long end of fixed income markets; since November 2024, there have been over $5 billion in outflows from longer-term US Treasury ETFs vs. $69 billion of inflows into short-term US Treasury ETFs.21

Looking forward, we continue to believe the ‘belly’ may outperform the long end. The continued normalization of term premia and concerns over the longer-term fiscal profile of the U.S. have added to the negative outlook for longer-duration bonds. By contrast, the ‘belly’ is one of the steepest parts of the yield curve and with our focus on income and carry, we believe that the allure of returns from rolldown only improves the risk/return of the 3- to 7-year sector. We see this sector of the curve as offering a mix of downside insulation while also participating in the benefits of modest duration, especially at a time when the Fed is likely to restart their easing cycle. Inflation expectations have remained elevated22 and we prefer shorter-dated inflation linked bonds to seek to capture upside inflation risk in the near term via exposure to real rates.

We believe corporate credit investors should focus on income and carry rather than expecting significant spread tightening. Credit spreads look tight, but all-in yields have remained attractive, thanks to elevated risk-free rates. We favor selectively moving down in credit quality to seek more carry. Despite resuming the easing cycle, we believe the Fed will likely keep interest rates above neutral for longer. We see this providing an opportunity for investors to use active management strategies to seek to harness yield from high quality borrowers across diversified sectors in the fixed income markets.

International government bonds may offer an attractive alternative for U.S. investors. A portfolio of hedged, local-currency bonds from lower-coupon countries has often offered superior returns versus higher-yielding US Treasuries — thanks to the contribution that the currency hedge can make to total return.23 So even while 10-year government bonds issued by Germany, France and Italy may have lower yields in EUR terms, when that is swapped back into U.S. dollars, the realized yields have been higher than the yield on a US 10-year Treasury.24 Given the integration of global credit markets and similar structural issues confronting the outlook for both U.S. and European rate investors, we also prefer the 3- to 7-year portion of global bond yield curves.

Figure 4: Comparative 10-year government bond yields on a currency-hedged basis

© 2025 BlackRock, Inc. All rights reserved.

24 Bloomberg. Reference indices are the German, French, Italian, and U.S. 10-year bond instruments, using foreign currency conversion from Euros to U.S. dollars. As of July 31, 2025.

When the Fund sells call options on a large cap equity index, it receives a premium but it takes on the risk that these options may reduce any profit from increases in the market value of the long equity positions held by the Fund. Any such reduction in profits would be the difference between the payoff of the call option and the premium received. The Fund would also retain the risk of loss if the long equity positions decline in value. The premiums received from the options may not be sufficient to offset any losses sustained from the long equity positions. Factors that may influence the value of the options generally include the underlying asset’s price, interest rates, dividends, the actual and implied volatility levels of the underlying asset’s price, and the remaining time until the options expire, among others. The value of the options written by the Fund typically do not increase or decrease at the same rate as the underlying asset’s price on a day-to-day basis due to these factors.

The Fund's use of derivatives may reduce the Fund's returns and/or increase volatility and subject the Fund to counterparty risk, which is the risk that the other party in the transaction will not fulfill its contractual obligation. data.title: VGT Is Not A Victim Of Nvidia
data.description:
What if I told you that information technology is not made only of chipmakers? Am I too banal? Just as it is not made only of NVDA.

Simply, taking as reference one of the ETFs in my opinion most representative, Vanguard Information Technology ETF (NYSEARCA:VGT), inside there are 319 holdings, and if we think about it carefully, it is NVDA that depends on them, not the other way around.

In these cases, one of those mantras I grew up with in my studies always comes to mind, repeated by McKinsey; paraphrasing: if you focus on the EPS of the quarter you lose sight of the "total dimension".

And analyzing in cascade, starting from the AI players, NVDA’s clients, up to NVDA itself, in VGT we discover a different truth from public opinion, focused on the fear of “a bubble.”

A genuine market, with good prospects in terms of EPS, which however has to deal with extreme valuations. One of those cases, in my opinion, with a HOLD rating.

What is VGT

For me it is a low-cost reference point for the US equity market of information technology.

Its representativeness lies behind the index it replicates: the MSCI US Investable Market Index (IMI)/Information Technology 25/50 (applies IRS constraints), which covers large, mid and small cap of the US IT sector. The replication is in the TER, equal to 0.09% and a bid/ask spread of 0.03%, with a very low turnover, of 13%. The result, one of the highest AUM, equal to $144.89B.

Holding distribution

It has over 31% in semiconductors, then 37-38% in software, the rest in Technology Hardware, Storage & Peripherals.

For a median market cap of $1.4 T that shows how, although small caps are present, they are naturally a residual part of the portfolio. What is immediately noticeable, on which to pay attention? Here NVDA weighs almost 19% of the total market cap. Then the top 10, about 60%.

Which makes the strong diversification of the number of holdings it boasted, about 319, less impactful compared simply to other ETFs like the classic XLK.

The fundamental result is in fact in line with other less diversified ETFs: an earnings growth rate of 31.3%, an ROE of 40.7% and a P/E of 39.9x.

Attention to NVDA

Although 18% is in NVDA, and if the rhetoric of NVDA as a barometer of the economy is true, the question arises spontaneously: what precious insight does the latest quarterly report give us? Something distinctive? There was no “wow effect”, but neither a slowdown.

The fear? that instead a slowdown could arrive with time: if in 2025 it was growing at +151%/+122% pace, talking about +49/+53% is reductive.

Well, let me say … it is not reductive. Widening the time horizon, and taking NVDA’s revenue tree, everything is proceeding according to plan. Indeed, the de-risking in the guidance due to the exclusion of China, and the buybacks for 60B shares, leave room for positive surprises in the coming quarters at the EPS level.

But the news focuses on the classic negative “what if”: “what if it were not so?”

A divergent opinion

If NVDA will not maintain sufficient growth rates, in my opinion, it will certainly not be NVDA’s balance sheet or guidance to say so.

The focus, in my opinion, must be shifted from NVDA to those who instead purchase NVDA’s products, an opinion on which Sam Altman (OpenAI) himself reflects: the hyperscalers, which are predominantly found in VGT, categorizable (aggregatively) in the software segment, a relevant component inside VGT. And it is here that the “what if” finds its maximum expression:

"Phase 2"

A bit like what Sam Altman calls, “phase 2”: where, after the tools to generate AI are distributed, a certain monetization on the software side will be needed (basically those who made the investments and made NVDA earn). And the size of this monetization of the hyperscalers will define the capital allocation of tomorrow. Like in the 1800s in the gold rush: if only those who “sell shovels” to mine gold make money, the miners will stop buying them, and the mechanism clogs.

Here’s what to look at (in my opinion)

If you follow this rhetoric, the focus should go to the software segment, which still weighs over 37% inside VGT. To check the state of health, I personally look at the guidance of the big tech software. In this sense, it helps a lot to move to the aggregate growth estimates of FactSet, which follow S&P 500 classified GICS Information Technology (earning weight), instead of using those of Vanguard.

The current data show that in Q2, backward-looking, even the software had an EPS growth of 19%, and a revenue growth of 16%, above the average of the S&P 500. The forward-looking data, FactSet reports aggregate (for the entire info. tech. market) positive forecasts for 2025 and 2026. But if you follow the expectations on the leaders of the VGT "software segment", the expectations remain positive as well. And this can be deduced from the analysis of the top ten, as defined in this table.

Therefore, I do not expect overall a bubble scenario. Earnings are there, and AI investments are expected to grow: infra spending by the end of the decade (3-4T).

Risk

On the other side, there is the forward P/E. If earnings do not exceed expectations, these forward P/Es will need more time to be digested by the market.

Therefore, we can say that the expected returns of the tech sector decrease compared to the past. Simply, there is no positive margin of safety. This makes VGT an ETF particularly susceptible to potential (negative) catalysts.

Conclusion

To conclude, shifting the focus from NVDA and the risk of an earnings slowdown to the software component, the guidance does not highlight in my opinion imbalances that could lead to future negative surprises. On the contrary, going back to NVDA, the de-risking and the buybacks play in favor of positive EPS surprises. Therefore, overall, net of unexpected catalysts, potentially dangerous given that the forward P/Es are very high, VGT in my opinion maintains the right rating of HOLD.

data.title: DDC Enterprise: IPO Money Burned, Bitcoin To The Rescue?
data.description:
You might have been fooled by this stock—it's definitely not a food products company anymore.

DDC Enterprise (NYSE:DDC) filed for an IPO two years ago to raise capital for its ready-to-cook meal business in China—but forget about meat.

Now it's all about cooking Bitcoins.

Those who have been my readers for a while will know that earlier this year I covered a similar story with Top Win International, a watch reseller that took advantage of the cash it was sitting on after its IPO and transitioned to a Bitcoin treasury.

Shortly afterward, it changed its ticker to AsiaStrategy (SORA).

And besides Bitcoin, do you know what else is similar between them? Neither was working with their previous business models.

A quick look at DDC Enterprise's financial statements tells us:

  1. Stagnant revenues, even after the IPO;
  2. SG&A covering all reported gross profit, resulting in negative EBIT since FY 2020;
  3. Significant net loss, reaching an EPS of -$14.95 in FY 2024;
  4. Negative OCF, increased by certain fluctuations in operating assets. Consequently, in every year since FY 2020, DDC has not reported a single positive FCF, increasing its long-term debt every year to protect its cash.

So, as you can probably guess, much like the former Top Win, DDC Enterprise looks like an easy candidate to turn into a Bitcoin treasury. It raised $33 million in its IPO, but was quickly burning through it in its core business anyway.

Where They Are and Where They Want to Go

Right now, there are two sources tracking how much BTC DDC has on its books.

The first one, from August 25, notes they picked up another 200 BTC, pushing their total stash to 888 BTC—worth about $98.95 million as of August 28. Since their average price is $107,447, they are up +3.7%.

Basically, the same press release tells us they have 1,000 shares for 0.106853 BTC.

Doing some basic math, we can say that DDC has approximately 8.3 million shares.

The second source puts DDC's stash even higher—at 1,008 BTC. That's 120 more than what the last press release mentioned, bringing the total value to about $112.33 million. In this case, the average price per BTC was $103,878, which tells us their position is approximately +7.4%.

Based on the 8.3 million shares, this second source indicates that DDC has 0.12124 BTC per 1,000 shares.

And where do they want to go?

According to the CEO, the goal is to reach 5,000 BTC within 36 months. Based on this statement at the end of Q1 FY 2025, DDC intends to reach this goal by mid-FY 2028.

That makes things way simpler—unlike Top Win, we actually know how much BTC they've got and where they're aiming. Right now, DDC sits as the 42nd largest Bitcoin treasury worldwide, and if they push that up to 5,000 BTC, they'd break into the Top 20.

Modeling Some Scenarios for DDC

Now that we have our premises on the table:

  • 888/1,008 BTC in treasury today;
  • 5,000 BTC by FY 2028;
  • 8.3 million shares.

Currently, DDC has somewhere between 0.000107 and 0.000121 BTC per share today (depending on which source you're looking at). At the current BTC price, we'd be looking at somewhere between $11.95 and $13.50.

Essentially, it's trading at no premium to the BTC it owns—or, in the worst-case scenario, at a very small premium.

Looking ahead to FY 2028 and estimating dilution of up to 12 million shares (to finance these purchases), we could imagine something close to 0.0004167 BTC per share.

If we target BTC at $150,000 three years from now, we'd be talking about $62.50 per share. Applying a 1.5x NAV, this could rise to as high as $93.

But remember, we're talking about the future. We need to bring this value into the present.

VCs generally use discount rates that vary between 30-50%, and that's what I'll use here:

  • 30%: $32.50;
  • 40%: $24.20;
  • 50%: $18.40.

Of course, there are a lot of moving parts here. Beyond the discount rate, we're talking about different BTC price scenarios for FY 2028 ($100K? $200K?), how much dilution they'll need to fund those buys, and even whether they can actually hit that 5,000 BTC mark in the first place.

Applying a conservative average between the lower and middle ends of the forecasts for the discount rate, we have a price target of approximately $21.30.

Look, that's an upside of 56.6% in three years. It sounds interesting, but honestly, I would prefer to buy BTC directly and self-custody it.

That said, my skepticism isn’t about Bitcoin itself—it’s about the company’s long-term strategy. At the end of the day, this is still a Chinese company that could easily end up in Washington’s crosshairs and face a delisting if tensions escalate.

For now, I’m sticking with a ‘Hold’—the upside is clear, but so are the risks.

data.title: U.S. ETF Growth Easily Outpaces Market Gains Since 2020
data.description:
With the recent news that there are now more ETFs than stocks in the US, we wanted to provide an update on the dollar amounts that have flowed into ETFs so far this decade.

Since the end of 2019, exchange-traded products (ETPs) in the U.S. - primarily ETFs - have exploded in size, outpacing even the impressive rise of the S&P 500.

The first chart below highlights just how powerful the trend has been: while the S&P 500’s total return has surged about +120% since the start of 2020, US ETF assets under management (AUM) have climbed an even steeper +177%. That gap shows that ETFs have not just grown alongside the market; they’ve attracted a massive wave of fresh capital from investors seeking low-cost, diversified, and flexible investment vehicles.

The second chart below underscores this growth in dollar terms. According to Bloomberg, U.S. ETF assets have ballooned from around $4 trillion at the start of 2020 to a record $12.3 trillion today. While markets have had their ups and downs over the past five years, the long-term trajectory of ETF adoption has been relentlessly upward. This expansion is being fueled not only by equity gains but also by the sheer volume of new inflows as investors of all sizes, retail to institutional, continue to shift from mutual funds and other structures into ETFs. Put simply, ETFs are no longer a niche product... they are the market. With their assets now equal to more than half of U.S. GDP, their influence on trading flows, liquidity, and even market structure will only grow. And with ETF growth running far ahead of the broader equity market, this wave of adoption shows no sign of slowing down.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Core PCE Inflation Rises 2.9% In July, As Expected
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The Fed’s preferred inflation gauge, the core PCE price index, rose 2.9% year-over-year in July. This was consistent with the forecast and marked an acceleration from June's 2.8% reading. On a monthly basis, the core index was up 0.3%, as expected.

The headline index was up 2.6% year-over-year. This was consistent with the forecast and marked a slight pickup from June. On a monthly basis, the index was up 0.3%, as expected.

PCE Price Index

Personal consumption expenditures (PCE) measures and tracks changes for all domestic personal consumption. Core PCE measures the changes in personal consumption of less food and energy, making it less volatile than the headline PCE. The PCE Price Index is calculated using PCE data and is a key way to measure changes in purchasing trends and inflation.

The adjacent thumbnail gives us a close-up of the trend in YoY core PCE since January 2012. The first string of red data points highlights the 12 consecutive months when core PCE hovered in a narrow range around its interim low. The second string highlights the lower range from late 2014 through 2015. Core PCE shifted higher in 2016 with a decline in 2017, 2019, and 2020, with a major jump in 2022.

The following chart shows the monthly year-over-year change in the Personal Consumption Expenditures price index since 2000. An overlay of the core PCE price index is also included, with the highlighted line on the chart representing the 2% benchmark for core inflation.

For a long-term perspective, here are the same two metrics spanning five decades.

Inflation: Fed's 2% Target

The Fed traditionally uses the PCE Price Index as their preferred inflation gauge; however, another common measure of inflation is the Consumer Price Index. Read our analysis on how these two inflationary measures stack up against each other.

In their latest meeting, the Fed held rates steady at 4.25-4.50%, as expected. This marked the fifth straight meeting the Fed has left rates unchanged. The statement from the meeting revealed the Committee believes inflation remains "somewhat elevated" but that they are strongly committed to returning inflation to its 2% objective.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: JQUA: I Prefer This ETF Over SCHG Due To Concentration Risks
data.description:
The concentration risk is growing in this market as the mega-cap tech stocks just keep rising. This has been great for the stock market and for investors, but many portfolios and ETF's are heavily weighted to tech stocks now. This heavy concentration is increasing risks and many investors might not realize how much their retirement or other investment accounts now rely on just a handful of stocks. At this point, the 7 largest stocks in the S&P 500 Index (SPY) now represent about one-third of the total market valuation, and Nvidia (NVDA) alone now represents about 8% of this major index. Part of what we learn when it comes to investing is to diversify. But, this important tenet of investing seems to be going by the wayside and at some point the market might humble investors with a market correction that will remind them why diversification is so important.

The Schwab U.S. Large-Cap Growth ETF (SCHG) is popular with investors and I like it also–and what is not to like since it has performed so well over the past few years. However, much of this performance has been due to big gains in a handful of tech stocks, so much so that I believe it is now too concentrated and potentially risky. In terms of its top holdings, Nvidia now represents nearly 12% of the portfolio. Microsoft (MSFT) is over 10% and Apple (AAPL) represents just over 9% of the portfolio holdings. Just these 3 top holdings represent more than 30% of the ETF. This type of concentration risk goes against some of the main reasons investors seek out ETF's and that is to have diversification and avoid single stock risks. The problem is that when you own an ETF that has a single stock that represents 12%, and the top 3 stocks represent over 30%, you are starting to take on some of that concentration risk that comes with buying single stocks. The other problem is that if Nvidia drops significantly, it could take down other big tech stocks, which is another reason why SCHG might be too risky for some investors. I believe SCHG is too elevated right now to be worth buying. However, I would buy it if there is a significant correction, but even then I would not make it a huge holding because it lacks the diversification I seek.

This brings me to an alternative ETF that still has many of the same portfolio holdings that SCHG has, but without the major concentration risk. The JP Morgan U.S. Quality Factor ETF (NYSEARCA:JQUA) could be an ideal investment for those that want leading tech stocks as top holdings, but with far more diversification and reduced dependence on just a handful of mega-cap tech stocks. Let's take a closer look below:

The Chart

As the chart below shows, JQUA has rallied to new 52-week highs recently. The 50-day moving average is $60.40 and the 200-day moving average is just over $58. The stock is a bit above these key support levels now, so I would wait for pullbacks if I were starting a new position.

Top Ten Holdings For JQUA

As the portfolio holdings that are provided by Seeking Alpha show below, JQUA does not have heavy concentration risk, since no single stock represents more than 2.5% of the portfolio. And yet, this ETF still has many of the same top portfolio holdings that SCHG does, with stocks like Nvidia, Apple, Microsoft and many more.

Top 10 Holdings

Let's Consider A Potential Rotation That Is Less Favorable to Nvidia And Tech/Momentum Stocks

While heavy tech stock and Nvidia concentration have benefited ETF's like SCHG, I think it is time to consider a forward-looking assessment whereby momentum and AI stocks cool off. A market rotation could occur soon which could benefit stocks and sectors that have been left behind in the AI and tech rally. Part of this thesis is based on the way Nvidia has traded after the company reported earnings, which is down, and tech stocks in general also fell. This could be a sign of cracks in the AI and tech trade, and it could even lead to a rotation into sectors that have reasonable valuations, such as energy and healthcare. I am concerned with the high valuations of stocks like Nvidia and many mega-cap tech stocks. It is getting to the point where 2 or 3 stocks could decline and take many ETF's and even the S&P 500 Index much lower.

When you consider that Nvidia alone now represents about 8% of the S&P 500 Index, and nearly 12% of the portfolio for SCHG, this represents a concentration risk that could be excessive. But, it is even more concerning when you consider that Nvidia also has major concentration risks when it comes to its business. A recent CNBC article points out that Nvidia's top two customers represented about 39% of its revenues for Q2. If even one of those customers decides to cut back on AI capex spending, this could have major negative implications for Nvidia, and if both customers cut back, it will be even worse. The problem is that if Nvidia shares plunge, it is likely to pull down other tech stocks with it. I believe this is a situation where JQUA will outperform SCHG and the major indexes because it has only a fraction of the exposure to Nvidia and it offers more diversification.

Comparing Total Returns

The JP Morgan U.S. Quality Factor ETF has generated strong returns and it has earned a 4 star rating overall and a gold medalist rating from Morningstar. It has an expense ratio of just 0.12% and a 30-day SEC yield of 1.16%. This ETF's inception date is 11-8-2017 and it has generated total returns of nearly 14% annually since inception. For the past year it has returned 13.43%, over the past 3 years it has averaged 16.37% annually, and 15.34% annually over the past 5 years. SCHG has outperformed this ETF, but as the saying goes, "past performance is no guarantee of future results". I believe the risks of SCHG could outweigh the rewards going forward and that JQUA clearly offers better diversification and yet very strong results that will let you sleep well at night. A $10,000 investment in JQUA at its inception in late 2017, is now worth nearly $28,000.

The Schwab U.S. Large-Cap Growth ETF has generated strong total returns. During the past year this ETF is up about 16%, and it has produced annual returns of around 27% over the past 3 years and around 18.7% annually over the past 5 years. Over the past ten years, it has generated average total returns of 16.39% annually. If you had put $10,000 in this fund about ten years ago, it could now be valued at roughly $45,422.60. This ETF has earned a 5 star rating from Morningstar.

When you compare the 5 year annual returns of 15.34% for JQUA and the 18.7% for SCHG, I believe that the difference is not enough to warrant the concentration risks that SCHG entails. I also believe that JQUA could outperform going forward whether the market drops or continues to advance because the diversification will help in a downturn and if the market goes higher, it could be because it broadens out to include more stocks and even laggards.

JQUA has provided strong risk-adjusted returns as its strategy focuses on mitigating downside risks while still capturing growth potential. This ETF has a history of successfully protecting against drawdowns of 15% or more and its screening for volatility enhances its risk-adjusted returns, especially during market downturns. JQUA's website states: "Going back to JQUA’s inception in November 2017, JQUA has successfully protected on the downside in all 3 periods of a 15% or more drawdown."

Where I Could Be Wrong

AI, robotics, and tech dominance could continue to drive stocks like Nvidia higher and if this continues, the high levels of investment in the mega-cap tech stocks in SCHG's portfolio will allow it to outperform. Fed rate cuts could be coming and could help take momentum-type tech stocks even higher which will help SCHG as well.

In Summary

Both of these ETF's have generated strong total returns for the past 5 years and could be ideal long-term investments. However, the market is at elevated levels right now and I believe JQUA is a more defensive and better choice for investors wanting diversification. Even so, I would give it a hold rating for now as it trades near 52-week highs, but I would buy on pullbacks.
